title: Scientific Advertising
subtitle: The definitive 1923 treatise on advertising as a measurable discipline — testing, reason-why copy, and the systematic methods that built billion-dollar brands
author: Claude C. Hopkins
publisher: Caxton Publishers (original); various reprints including Crown Publishers (1964), Bloombury (2007)
themes: Advertising as a science, not art — every claim must be tested and measured
Reason-why copy: sell benefits, not features
Coupon testing as the first rigorous marketing attribution method
The supremacy of the headline in ad performance
Long copy vs short copy — detail drives conviction and response
Product superiority as the most powerful sales argument
Service and education ahead of persuasion
The psychology of purchase: desire, fear, self-interest
Direct response as the only accountable form of advertising
awards: Called 'the father of modern advertising' by David Ogilvy
Enduring influence on direct-response marketing for 100+ years
excerpt: Scientific Advertising established advertising as a measurable, testable discipline rather than an art form driven by intuition. Claude C. Hopkins — the executive who turned Schlitz beer into a national brand and made Pepsodent a household name — codified the principles he used to generate billions in sales. Every modern direct-response marketer, copywriter, and growth hacker stands on his shoulders. This is not a book of clever slogans. It is a manual for making advertising accountable.
